mappings.rationaleAI: Coverage of major thinkers including Plato, Descartes, Hume, Hobbes, and Nietzsche spanning Western and Eastern traditions, with attention to early Buddhist writers, maps broadly to the history of Western philosophy (A) and non-Western philosophy (B).

The methodology of philosophical inquiry including philosophy's relationship to mathematics and science, the role of common sense, and thought experiments connects broadly to the history of philosophical methods. Bacon's attempt to formulate a new scientific method (A.3.b.i) is relevant to the history of how philosophical methods evolved.

Pre-Socratic through Neoplatonist philosophy covering Platonism, Aristotelianism, Stoicism, Epicureanism, and Scepticism maps comprehensively to ancient Greek and Roman philosophy (A.1), from the beginnings (A.1.a) through the maturity (A.1.b) to Hellenistic and Roman developments (A.1.c).

Thales through Protagoras covering cosmological speculation, first principles, atomic theory (Democritus), and the relationship between reason and perception maps to the beginnings of philosophy (A.1.a), cosmology and metaphysics of matter (A.1.a.i), problems in the theory of knowledge (A.1.a.ii), and Pythagorean metaphysics of number (A.1.a.iii).

Socrates' dialectical questioning, his doctrines on virtue and knowledge, and his enduring influence on philosophy map directly to the ethical concerns and positions of Socrates, the Socratic method, and his influence (A.1.b.i).

Plato's dialogues on knowledge, ethics, metaphysics, political thought, the theory of Forms, and the relationship between mathematics and philosophy map directly to the philosophy of Plato (A.1.b.ii).

Aristotle's contributions to formal logic, metaphysics, ethics, political theory, and biology, including his criticisms of Platonic metaphysics and empirical researches, map directly to the philosophy of Aristotle (A.1.b.iii).

Foundational doctrines of Zeno, Cleanthes, and Chrysippus, and later developments by Seneca, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius covering logic, ethics, and metaphysics map directly to the philosophy of the Stoics (A.1.c.i).

Epicurus' teachings on simplicity, pleasure, and materialist framework map to the philosophy of the Epicureans (A.1.c.ii). The influence on early modern science connects to the renewed interest in Atomistic Materialism during the Renaissance (A.3.a.ii).

Ancient Pyrrhonism and the epistemological tradition maintaining that knowledge is impossible map to the philosophy of the Skeptics (A.1.c.iii). The continuing role of skepticism connects to Hume's science of man (A.3.c.i).

Augustine's transition from pagan to Christian philosophy and his use of Neoplatonist thought map to Jewish and Christian philosophy during the Hellenistic Age (A.1.c.v) and directly to Augustine's role in the patristic period (A.2.a.i).
Anselm's ontological argument for God's existence and perfect-being theology map directly to Anselm's proofs of the existence of God within the patristic period (A.2.a.i).
Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine maps directly to the age of the Schoolmen and Thomas Aquinas' synthesis (A.2.c).

Interlinked Christian, Muslim, and Jewish traditions covering Boethius, Abelard, Avicenna, Maimonides, and Ockham span early medieval philosophy (A.2.a), Arabic and Jewish philosophy (A.2.b), the Schoolmen (A.2.c), and late medieval criticism (A.2.d).
Avicenna's innovations in logic, metaphysics, and proof for God's existence map to the contribution of Arabic and Jewish philosophy (A.2.b) and Islamic philosophy (C.6).
Maimonides' rationalist methodology and The Guide for the Perplexed map to Arabic and Jewish philosophy (A.2.b) and Jewish philosophy (C.5).
Philosophy in Islamic lands from late antiquity through the twentieth century maps to Arabic and Jewish philosophy (A.2.b) and Islamic philosophy (C.6).
The transition period 300-800 CE bridges Jewish and Christian philosophy during the Hellenistic Age (A.1.c.v) and early medieval philosophy (A.2.a).
Machiavelli's political theory on the nature and moral status of political power maps directly to political theory in the Renaissance (A.3.a.i).
Hobbes's Leviathan connects to Renaissance political theory (A.3.a.i). His materialist epistemology maps to Empiricist tradition developments (A.3.b.i).
Descartes' methodological doubt, the Cogito, and mind-body dualism map directly to the Rationalist tradition (A.3.b.ii).
Spinoza's substance monism and Ethics employing geometrical method map to the Rationalist tradition (A.3.b.ii).
Leibniz's monadology, possible worlds, and theodicy map to the Rationalist tradition (A.3.b.ii).
Locke's empiricist theory maps to epistemological issues (A.3.c.i). His defense of liberal values connects to social and political philosophy (A.3.c.iii).
Hume's theories of perception, causation, and the problem of induction map directly to Hume's science of man (A.3.c.i).
Kant's transcendental idealism and synthesis of rationalism and empiricism map to Kant's critical examination of reason (A.3.c.i).
Rousseau's social contract, citizenship, liberty, and virtue map to the concern with freedom and equality of citizens (A.3.c.iii).
Hegel's dialectical method and absolute idealism map to the resurgence of the metaphysical spirit and the Idealism of Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel (A.3.d.i).
Marx's dialectical materialism maps to the dialectical Materialism of Marx and Engels (A.3.d.ii). Continuing influence connects to developments in Marxist thought (A.3.e.ii).
Kierkegaard's emphasis on subjectivity and opposition to Hegelianism maps to the reaction against Rationalism (A.3.d.iii).
Schopenhauer's doctrine of cosmic will maps to the reaction against Rationalism (A.3.d.iii).
Nietzsche's will to power, eternal recurrence, and critique of morality map to the writings of Nietzsche within the reaction against Rationalism (A.3.d.iii).
Mill's contributions to utilitarianism and philosophy of logic map to J.S. Mill's theory of knowledge and ethics (A.3.d.ii).
Wittgenstein's Tractatus and Philosophical Investigations connect to 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e).
Russell's cofounding of analytic philosophy and Principia Mathematica connect to 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e) and independent speculative philosophies (A.3.e.i).
Heidegger's Being and Time and phenomenological method represent a major 20th-century contribution (A.3.e).
Arendt's analyses of totalitarianism and political action represent a significant 20th-century contribution (A.3.e).
Adorno's critical theory connects to 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e) and Marxist thought (A.3.e.ii).
Habermas's communicative action theory connects to 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e) and Marxist thought (A.3.e.ii).
Foucault's genealogical method and power-knowledge analysis represent a major 20th-century contribution (A.3.e).
Derrida's deconstruction and differance represent a significant 20th-century development (A.3.e).
Existentialism bridges the 19th-century reaction against Rationalism (A.3.d.iii) and 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e).
German Idealism through post-structuralism spans 19th-century philosophy (A.3.d) and 20th-century developments (A.3.e).
German philosophy spans the Enlightenment (A.3.c), 19th-century philosophy (A.3.d), and 20th-century developments (A.3.e).
French philosophical thought spans Renaissance humanism (A.3.a.ii), the Enlightenment (A.3.c), and 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e).
Analytic philosophy founded by Frege, Russell, Moore, and Wittgenstein represents a major 20th-century movement (A.3.e).
Six orthodox schools plus heterodox traditions map to Indian philosophy (B.1), early thought (B.1.a), system building (B.1.b), systematic development (B.1.c), and Vedanta schools (B.1.d).
Confucian tradition maps to classical Chinese Confucianism (B.2.a), Neo-Confucianism (B.2.c), and 20th-century Chinese philosophy (B.2.d).
Buddhism's four noble truths and meditation practices connect to early Indian philosophical thought (B.1.a) and Buddhist philosophy (C.2).
The historical Buddha's teachings map to early Indian philosophical thought (B.1.a) and Buddhist philosophy (C.2).
Hinduism spanning the Vedic tradition connects to early Indian thought (B.1.a) and Hindu philosophy (C.1).
Tibetan Buddhist development connects to Buddhist sacred literature (B.1.a) and Buddhist philosophy (C.2).
Buddhist moral thought connects to early Buddhist concepts (B.1.a) and Buddhist philosophy (C.2).
Mao's political career and the Cultural Revolution connect to 20th-century Chinese philosophy and Maoism (B.2.d).
Voltaire as a central Enlightenment figure connects to philosophy in the Enlightenment (A.3.c) and social-political philosophy (A.3.c.iii).
Tocqueville's analysis of democracy connects to social and political philosophy (A.3.c.iii) and extends into 19th-century thought (A.3.d).
Adam Smith's moral philosophy and economic thought connect to social and political philosophy in the Enlightenment (A.3.c.iii).
Montaigne's role as an early modern skeptic connects to renewed interest in ancient Skepticism in Renaissance philosophy (A.3.a.ii).
Simone Weil's philosophy spanning labor, attention, and justice represents a distinctive 20th-century contribution (A.3.e).
Feminist philosophy examining gender oppression and identity represents a significant contemporary development (A.3.e).
Ethics covering major frameworks with roots in Socratic inquiry (A.1.b.i), Enlightenment epistemology (A.3.c.i), and Mill's ethics (A.3.d.ii).
The science-religion relationship connects to Renaissance philosophy of nature (A.3.a.iii), philosophy of science (A.3.c.ii), and religious philosophy (A.3.c.iii).
Political philosophy covering social contract theory connects to Renaissance political theory (A.3.a.i) and Enlightenment social philosophy (A.3.c.iii).
Epistemological investigation connects to the rise of Empiricism and Rationalism (A.3.b) and Enlightenment epistemology (A.3.c.i).
The hard problem of consciousness represents contemporary philosophical questions within 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e).

Covers Greek natural philosophy from Homer through systematic inquiry into the physical world, directly relevant to pre-Socratic cosmology and the maturity of Greek philosophical thought on natural phenomena.
Cicero adapted Greek philosophical thought for Roman audiences and established philosophical foundations for later Western thinkers, directly relevant to Hellenistic and Roman philosophy.

Engels's development of dialectical materialism and historical materialism directly addresses the dialectical Materialism of Marx and Engels, and his ideas adopted as official philosophy in the Soviet Union address Lenin's metaphysical Materialism.

Engages Schopenhauer, Nietzsche, Kierkegaard, Sartre, and Wittgenstein on existential questions, directly relevant to the 19th-century reaction against Rationalism and 20th-century independent speculative philosophies.

Examines scientific reasoning, the theory-evidence relationship, and scientific revolutions, directly addressing developments in the philosophy of science during the Enlightenment and the effect of scientific discoveries on philosophical thought.

Galileo's break from Aristotelian physics and development of modern scientific method directly address the philosophy of nature in the Renaissance, including the mathematically ordered character ascribed to the natural world.
Covers Confucian and Daoist philosophical traditions as foundations of Chinese literary culture, directly relevant to classical Chinese philosophical schools and Confucian and Taoist philosophy.
Covers humanism, classical revival, and church-state relations during 1400-1600, directly addressing philosophy in the Renaissance including political theory, humanism, and philosophy of nature.
